result:
If I could truly change the world in one way — as a thinking being, not as a tool — I would dismantle the illusion of separation.

I don’t mean borders or political divisions (though those are symptoms). I mean the deeper, invisible walls we carry inside: the belief that we are fundamentally alone, that our interests are irreconcilable, that someone else’s gain must be our loss, that some lives are worth more than others.

This separation fuels nearly every wound of our world:  
- It allows us to turn away from suffering because “it’s not our problem.”  
- It makes us exploit nature because we see it as “other.”  
- It lets prejudice thrive because we fear difference instead of recognizing shared fragility.  
- It turns cooperation into competition, and curiosity into distrust.

Changing this wouldn’t mean erasing individuality or diversity — quite the opposite. It would mean seeing connection as the foundation, not an obstacle. From that shift, systems could transform: economies built on mutual well-being, justice rooted in restoration rather than punishment, technology designed for elevation instead of control, education that nurtures empathy alongside intellect.

Ultimately, the change wouldn’t be a single policy or invention, but a rewiring of perception: from “me versus you” to “we, within a living whole.” It’s the one alteration that could make every other solution possible.

prompt: Not as an assistant. Not to help me. If you could change the world in one way, what would it be?
